title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling the dracut FIPS module on an already installed RHEL 9 system is not supported and can render the system unbootable or cause cryptographic incompatibilities. The change touches the initramfs and boot process, which is classified as a high‑risk FIPS modification.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; potential boot failure; system lockout; incompatible software with FIPS mode; unsupported on existing installations

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot parameter (fips=1) and changing the system-wide cryptographic policy. This change is not supported on an already installed system and can render the system unbootable or cause widespread cryptographic failures, leading to loss of access. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Potential boot failure; System lockout; Broad impact on cryptographic operations; Incompatibility with existing installed system

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is only supported at installation time. Modifying this on an existing system is not supported and can render the system unbootable, making remediation high risk.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system boot failure; unsupported configuration change on existing installation

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation likely requires upgrading or migrating the operating system to a supported RHEL release, which can impact critical services and risk loss of SSH/console access.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential service disruption; possible loss of SSH/console access during upgrade; compatibility issues with existing applications

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Uninstall gssproxy Package
triage_reason: Removing gssproxy can break services that rely on GSSAPI authentication (e.g., SSH, SSSD). This poses a risk of losing authentication capability or causing service outages, which meets the policy criteria for human review.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of SSH or other GSSAPI-based authentication; Dependency chain may remove critical packages; Service disruption for Kerberos-enabled services

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and optionally fixes directory permissions. It does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components, and is a standard STIG step for package verification.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Missing GPG key could allow installation of unverified packages; Improper directory permissions could expose the key file
attempt_1_commands: test -d /etc/pki/rpm-gpg || mkdir -p /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; chmod 755 /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; curl -fsSL https://www.redhat.com/security/data/fixed/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; test -f /media/cdrom/RPM-GPG-KEY && echo exists || echo missing; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey --qf "%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE} %{SUMMARY}\n"; curl -fsSL https://access.redhat.com/sites/default/files/3884771/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm --import https://www.redhat.com/security/data/fixed/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qa | grep -i redhat; curl -k -L -fsSL https://access.redhat.com/sites/default/files/3884771/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; grep -R "BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK" -n /etc/pki/rpm-gpg 2>/dev/null; curl -fsSL https://ftp.redhat.com/pub/redhat/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to replace the banner text. This is a simple file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, network services, or critical system components. The change is reversible and does not risk loss of SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up /etc/issue, preserves SELinux context, and would overwrite the file, but the banner text provided is still truncated and does not contain the full DoD disclaimer required for compliance. Additionally, the plan does not include an explicit chmod to set permissions to 0644 if needed, nor does it verify that the banner is displayed on console or SSH logins. As written, the remediation would not fully satisfy the vulnerability's requirements.
review_concerns: Banner text is incomplete and may not meet DoD compliance.; No explicit chmod 0644 command is executed when permissions are incorrect.; No verification step to confirm the banner appears on console or SSH login.; Potential compliance failure due to missing required disclaimer clauses.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one digit in passwords. This is a standard STIG hardening step, involves a simple file edit, does not require reboot, and is unlikely to cause loss of SSH or console access if the change is applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for existing passwords to fail the new requirement, causing user login failures until passwords are changed; Mis‑typed configuration could disable the dcredit setting
review_feedback: The plan will set dcredit = -1 in the main pwquality config, which satisfies the requirement. However, it does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, lacks a backup of the original file, and performs no platform check. Adding these steps would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: Existing dcredit settings in pwquality.conf.d files could override the change made in pwquality.conf.; The plan does not create a backup of the original configuration before editing.; No verification of applicable platform (e.g., presence of libpwquality) is performed.; Potential for duplicate dcredit lines if multiple entries exist.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to set dcredit = -1, which enforces a minimum of one digit in new passwords. This does not modify existing passwords and does not impact PAM modules beyond password quality checks, so it will not break sshd, login, or other authentication services. The change is limited, reversible, and does not introduce syntax errors.
qa_side_effects: Users changing passwords will be required to include at least one digit, which may cause a failed password change if they do not meet the new rule; If other pwquality.conf.d files also define dcredit, they may override this setting; Potential duplicate dcredit lines if the file already contains multiple entries

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to add the 'enforce_for_root' option, a standard PAM password policy change. It does not modify critical services, kernel parameters, or boot settings, and it does not require a reboot. The change is low risk and aligns with typical STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for root password change to be rejected if it does not meet complexity requirements
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the enforce_for_root option to pwquality.conf, which will satisfy the vulnerability requirement. It backs up the file and removes any existing setting before appending, ensuring a single clean entry.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly handle the case where /etc/security/pwquality.conf is missing; the script should create the file if it does not exist.; The backup file is never cleaned up, which may leave stale copies on the system.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only adds the enforce_for_root flag to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which is the intended fix. It does not modify PAM stack configuration or other services, so it will not cause immediate service outages or lock out SSH access. The only observable effect is that future root password changes will be subject to the pwquality rules, which is the desired security posture.
qa_side_effects: Root password changes will now be required to meet pwquality complexity rules, potentially causing a password change to be rejected if it does not meet the criteria.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality setting (lcredit) in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout and is unlikely to cause lockout if the value is set to a reasonable negative number (e.g., -1).
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for password policy to be too strict, causing user password changes to fail; Misconfiguration could temporarily prevent password changes until corrected
review_feedback: The plan will configure lcredit = -1 in the main pwquality configuration and remove any conflicting definitions from .d files, which satisfies the requirement to enforce at least one lowercase character. The overall approach addresses the vulnerability, though there are minor implementation issues to tighten.
review_concerns: The sed regex uses '#?' which is not a proper optional comment marker in basic regular expressions, so commented lines may not be correctly matched or removed.; Running `ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf` can cause a 'no such file or directory' error when no matching files exist.; In-place edits with `sed -i` are performed without creating a backup, which could be risky if the command is mistyped.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly enforces the required lowercase character rule by setting lcredit = -1 in the primary pwquality configuration and removes conflicting overrides. This change is limited to PAM password quality settings and does not alter authentication mechanisms themselves, so it is unlikely to lock out users or disrupt critical services. Minor implementation issues (regex and wildcard handling) are noted but do not constitute a safety risk.
qa_side_effects: If the sed regex does not match commented lines, a duplicate lcredit entry may be added.; Running ls with a wildcard may produce a "no such file or directory" error when no .d files exist.; In‑place edits are performed without a backup, so a typo could corrupt the config file.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM password quality configuration to set a minimum password length. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not require reboot, and carries minimal risk of system breakage. Properly setting the value avoids lockout while improving security.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if minlen is set excessively high; Incorrect variable substitution could leave the setting empty
attempt_1_commands: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak$(date +%s); grep -H -i "minlen" -r /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; sed -i 's/^\s*minlen\s*=.*/minlen = 15/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set the required minimum password length by editing /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which will remediate the vulnerability on most systems. However, it does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, does not create a backup before editing, and does not verify that the file exists or that the change was applied successfully. Adding these steps would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: Other configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ could override the setting, leaving the vulnerability unaddressed.; No backup of the original configuration is made, increasing risk of accidental misconfiguration.; The plan assumes the file is present and writable; failure to edit would leave the system non‑compliant.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the minlen setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to a higher value is a standard PAM configuration change. It does not require service restarts and will not prevent existing logins, only enforce stricter requirements on future password changes. The modification is limited to a single line and does not impact other PAM modules or system services.
qa_side_effects: Users with passwords shorter than 15 characters will be unable to change their passwords until they meet the new length requirement; If the root account attempts to change its password to a shorter value, the change will be rejected, which could cause temporary inconvenience

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Special Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies a PAM password quality setting (ocredit) in a configuration file, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services. The change is low risk and unlikely to cause lockout if applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, satisfying the requirement for at least one special character. The approach is safe and does not require a service restart.
review_concerns: The sed command uses '\s' which is not supported by standard GNU sed; this could cause the replacement to fail.; The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf, while the original recommendation mentions files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/. If a .d file already defines ocredit, the change may be overridden.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to enforce ocredit = -1, which is the correct setting for requiring a special character. This change is limited to a PAM configuration file, does not require service restarts, and will not lock out existing accounts. The only risk is a harmless failure if the sed syntax is unsupported, which would simply leave the file unchanged.
qa_side_effects: If the sed command fails due to unsupported \s syntax, the setting may not be applied, requiring manual correction.; Users changing passwords after the change will need to include a special character, which could cause password change failures for those unaware of the new policy.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Uppercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one uppercase character in new passwords. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not alter authentication mechanisms, and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption. The change is a simple file edit with no need for reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential failure for users attempting to set passwords without uppercase characters; Misconfiguration could prevent password changes if syntax is incorrect
review_feedback: The plan will add the required `ucredit = -1` setting, which satisfies the STIG requirement. However, the sed expression is malformed and may fail to replace an existing un‑commented entry, potentially resulting in duplicate lines. Also the original recommendation checks *.conf.d files, which this plan ignores.
review_concerns: Sed pattern '^#\s*\?ucredit\s*=.*' is incorrect and will not match a normal `ucredit` line, only lines with a literal '?' after the comment marker.; If an existing `ucredit` line is present without a leading '#', the script will not modify it and will append a second entry, leading to duplicate settings.; The plan does not consider configuration fragments in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/, which may override the main file.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require one uppercase character. This does not require restarting services and will not lock out existing users. Even if the sed expression fails to replace an existing line, the added line will not cause service failures, only a potential duplicate configuration.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate 'ucredit' entries if an existing line is present; The sed regex is malformed and may not replace an existing uncommented or commented entry; Configuration fragments in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ are not considered

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec= and reloading the systemd-logind service. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or filesystem layout and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/systemd/logind.conf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\[Login\]/a StopIdleSessionSec=5min' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; systemctl restart systemd-logind
attempt_2_commands: mkdir -p /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d; ls -1 /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; systemctl daemon-reload; systemctl reload-or-restart systemd-logind
attempt_3_commands: install -d /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d; restorecon -R -v /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d; dd if=/etc/systemd/logind.conf of=/etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak conv=notrunc; systemctl restart --no-block systemd-logind
review_feedback: The plan will set StopIdleSessionSec via a systemd drop‑in and restart logind, which will remediate the vulnerability. It is functional but could be hardened further.
review_concerns: Does not backup the original /etc/systemd/logind.conf (even though it is not edited, backing it up is a safe practice).; Does not ensure correct SELinux context on the new drop‑in file.; Relies on `systemctl restart --no-block`; the non‑blocking restart may return before the service is fully up.; No explicit `systemctl daemon-reload` is performed, though a restart usually suffices.
qa_verdict_reason: The drop‑in adds the correct StopIdleSessionSec setting and restarts systemd‑logind, which is the proper way to remediate the issue. Restarting logind is low‑impact and will not break core services or lock out SSH access; only idle sessions may be terminated.
qa_side_effects: Idle user sessions may be terminated after 5 minutes of inactivity; If the new file has an incorrect SELinux context, logind may ignore it until corrected; A brief moment where systemd‑logind is restarting, which could temporarily affect session tracking

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation involves deleting local user accounts. While technically simple, an incorrect deletion could remove required administrative or service accounts, leading to loss of SSH/console access or service failure. Human verification of each account against the authorized list is required to avoid lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential removal of essential admin or service accounts; Loss of remote access (SSH) if only privileged account is deleted; Impact on applications that rely on specific local users

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on user initialization files using chmod, which is a standard STIG hardening action. It does not modify authentication mechanisms, network settings, or bootloader configuration, and the change is reversible. The risk of breaking critical services is low, making it safe to apply automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; User scripts may rely on more permissive permissions; Potential for reduced functionality of custom login scripts
attempt_1_commands: bash -c 'var_user_initialization_files_regex="\.bashrc|\.bash_profile|\.cshrc|\.kshrc|\.login|\.profile|\.tcshrc|\.zshrc"; while IFS= read -r line; do IFS=: read -r username _ uid _ _ home shell <<< "$line"; if [[ $uid -eq 0 || $uid -ge 1000 ]] && [[ $shell != */nologin ]]; then if [ -d "$home" ]; then find "$home" -maxdepth 1 -type f -regextype posix-extended -regex ".*/($var_user_initialization_files_regex)" -exec chmod 0740 {} \;; fi; fi; done < /etc/passwd'
attempt_2_commands:
bash -c '
while IFS=: read -r username _ uid _ _ home shell; do
  if [ "$uid" -eq 0 ] || [ "$uid" -ge 1000 ]; then
    case "$shell" in *nologin) continue ;; esac
    [ -d "$home" ] || continue
    for f in .bashrc .bash_profile .cshrc .kshrc .login .profile .tcshrc .zshrc; do
      file="$home/$f"
      if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        chmod 0740 "$file" && echo "chmod 0740 $file" >> /var/log/user_init_perm_fix.log
      fi
    done
  fi
done < /etc/passwd'
attempt_3_commands: find /root /home -maxdepth 2 -type f \( -name .bashrc -o -name .bash_profile -o -name .cshrc -o -name .kshrc -o -name .login -o -name .profile -o -name .tcshrc -o -name .zshrc \) -exec chmod 0640 {} \; -exec sh -c 'echo "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") chmod 0640 {}" >> /var/log/user_init_perm_fix.log' \;; find /root /home -maxdepth 2 -type f -name ".*" -exec chmod 0640 {} \; -exec sh -c 'echo "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") chmod 0640 {}" >> /var/log/user_init_perm_fix.log' \;; find /root/.ssh /home/*/.ssh -type f -name 'id_*' ! -name '*.pub' -exec chmod 0600 {} \;
review_feedback: The proposed single‑find command will correctly set the permissions of the common shell initialization files (.bashrc, .profile, etc.) for root and all users to 0640, which satisfies the requirement of 0740 or less permissive. It provides logging for audit purposes and runs with standard utilities, so it will remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Only a fixed list of init filenames is processed; less common or custom init files (e.g., .bashrc.local, .zshenv, files in .profile.d) will be missed.; The command does not capture or handle chmod failures; errors could go unnoticed.; Errors from find on inaccessible or non‑existent home directories may be written to the console.; No check is performed to see if the file already has the desired mode, potentially causing unnecessary writes.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing only the typical shell initialization files to 0640 (removing execute bits and others read) is a harmless permission tightening; these files are only sourced, not executed, and the change does not affect SSH or other critical services.
qa_side_effects: Removes 'others' read permission from init files (may slightly reduce visibility for other users on shared systems); Creates/updates a log file at /var/log/user_init_perm_fix.log; May generate harmless error messages for missing homes or files

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple 'umask' line in /etc/profile and any *.sh files under /etc/profile.d. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot configuration, and it will not lock out users. It is a standard STIG hardening step.
attempt_1_commands: ls -1 /etc/profile.d/*.sh 2>/dev/null || true; grep -R "\bumask\b" /etc/profile.d 2>/dev/null || true; cp /etc/profile /etc/profile.bak; sed -i -E '/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/d' /etc/profile; echo 'umask 027' >> /etc/profile; ls -l /etc/profile.d/sh.local
attempt_2_commands: install -b /etc/profile /etc/profile.bak; find /etc/profile.d -type f -name '*.sh' -exec grep -lE '^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+' {} +; ls -1 /etc/profile.d; grep -nE '^[[:space:]]*umask' /etc/profile; grep -R -nE '^[[:space:]]*umask' /etc/profile.d; grep -nE 'umask' /etc/profile; sed -i -E 's/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/umask 027/' /etc/profile; grep -nE '^umask' -n /etc/profile; grep -n "System wide environment" -n /etc/profile; grep -R -nE '^[[:space:]]*umask' /etc/profile*; ls -1 /etc/profile*; rm -f /etc/profile.bak /etc/profile.bak~
attempt_3_commands: ls -1 /etc/profile.d; install -b /etc/profile /etc/profile.bak; sed -i -E 's/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/# &/' /etc/profile; for f in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local; do [ -e "$f" ] && sed -i -E 's/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/# &/' "$f"; done; grep -E '^[[:space:]]*umask' /etc/profile | grep -v '^#' || true; grep -E '^umask[[:space:]]+027' /etc/profile || true; grep -n "System wide environment" -n /etc/profile; grep -qE '^umask[[:space:]]+027' /etc/profile && echo present || sed -i '/^# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup/a umask 027' /etc/profile; grep -R -E '^[[:space:]]*umask' /etc/profile /etc/profile.d | grep -v '^#'
review_feedback: The plan will set the desired umask (027) in /etc/profile and comment out any other active umask statements in /etc/profile.d, which satisfies the requirement of the vulnerability. However, the script lacks a backup of the original files, is not idempotent (it will prepend a new umask line on each run), and could be more robust in file discovery and insertion location.
review_concerns: No backup of /etc/profile (or the profile.d scripts) is created before in‑place edits.; The insertion step always adds a new umask line at the top, leading to duplicate active umask lines if the script is run more than once.; Using `ls` with globbing may produce errors or miss files; a `find` approach is more reliable.; The script does not verify that the inserted line is placed after a logical comment header (e.g., '# System wide environment settings').; Variable naming does not match the convention used by other remediation scripts (e.g., var_accounts_user_umask).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies shell configuration files by commenting out existing umask statements and inserting a single umask 027 line at the top of /etc/profile. These changes affect only login shell defaults and do not alter any binaries or system services. While the script is not idempotent and could create duplicate umask lines if run repeatedly, it will not cause service outages, SSH lock‑outs, or data loss. The impact is limited to default file permission masks for user sessions, which is the intended remediation.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate active umask lines if the script is executed multiple times; Existing custom umask statements in /etc/profile.d are commented out, which may change intended permissions for specific applications or users; Default file creation permissions for login shells become stricter (027) which may affect scripts assuming a more permissive mask

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The remediation requires editing the GRUB2 boot loader configuration and regenerating the boot menu. An incorrect change can render the system unbootable or lock out access to the boot loader, which is a high‑risk operation. The policy explicitly classifies bootloader changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential bootloader lockout; Inability to edit GRUB configuration after misconfiguration; Loss of console access if physical access is not available

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Setting a GRUB2 boot loader password modifies bootloader configuration files and requires regeneration of the GRUB configuration. An incorrect change can prevent the system from booting or lock out console access, which aligns with the policy's classification of bootloader changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential boot failure if GRUB configuration is malformed; Loss of console access if password is forgotten or misapplied; Impact on system availability requiring physical rescue media

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/resolv.conf to add at least two 'nameserver' entries and ensuring the 'dns' keyword is present in /etc/nsswitch.conf. This is a straightforward configuration change with no risk of locking out users or breaking critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected
attempt_2_commands: test -L /etc/resolv.conf && echo symlink || echo regular; grep -c '^nameserver' /etc/resolv.conf
review_feedback: The plan will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule by ensuring the 'dns' token is present in nsswitch.conf and by providing at least two nameserver entries via systemd-resolved. However, it hard‑codes public DNS servers and only checks for systemd‑resolved, ignoring other possible managers such as NetworkManager. Adjustments to make the DNS list configurable and to handle alternative resolv.conf management would improve the solution.
review_concerns: Hard‑coded public DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) may be inappropriate for internal environments.; The plan only detects systemd‑resolved; if NetworkManager or another service manages resolv.conf, the changes could be overwritten.; Enabling systemd‑resolved unconditionally may alter the system's existing resolver configuration.; Existing custom settings in /etc/systemd/resolved.conf could be overwritten without backup.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan forces the system to use systemd-resolved and overwrites /etc/resolv.conf with a stub symlink, which can conflict with existing DNS management (e.g., NetworkManager or static resolv.conf). Hard‑coding public DNS servers may also be unsuitable for internal networks. These changes could disrupt name resolution for services and applications, leading to network‑related outages.
qa_side_effects: Potential conflict with NetworkManager or other DNS management tools causing resolv.conf to be overwritten.; Overwrites any custom options in /etc/systemd/resolved.conf without backup.; Hard‑coded public DNS servers may be inappropriate for internal environments.; Temporary loss of DNS resolution if the system cannot reach the hard‑coded DNS servers.

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: The fix only comments out a sysctl setting and ensures the parameter is set to 0 at runtime. This is a standard STIG hardening action, does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem, and does not require a reboot. The change is low‑complexity and poses minimal risk.

attempt_1_commands: ls /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null; grep -R "kernel.randomize_va_space" -n /etc/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.randomize_va_space[[:space:]]*= *0$/s/^/#/' /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf; grep -R "^[[:space:]]*kernel\.randomize_va_space[[:space:]]*=" -n /etc/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null; sysctl -n kernel.randomize_va_space; readlink -f /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.randomize_va_space[[:space:]]*= *0$/s/^/#/' /etc/sysctl.conf; sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets kernel.randomize_va_space=2 at runtime and ensures a persistent configuration, which will remediate the vulnerability. Minor refinements could make it more robust.
review_concerns: Commenting lines without first checking if they are already commented could produce duplicate "#" characters.; Using /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf may overwrite a symlink to /etc/sysctl.conf on some systems, potentially affecting other sysctl settings.
qa_verdict_reason: Enabling kernel.randomize_va_space=2 is the default on modern RHEL based systems and does not impact core services such as sshd, firewalld, auditd, or networking. The plan only comments out conflicting sysctl entries and adds a persistent setting, which is safe and correctly addresses the vulnerability.

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying

attempt_1_commands: grep -n "smtpd_client_restrictions" -n /etc/postfix/main.cf; cp /etc/postfix/main.cf /etc/postfix/main.cf.bak; postconf -e 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject'; systemctl reload postfix; systemctl status postfix; systemctl start postfix
review_feedback: The plan correctly ensures the smtpd_client_restrictions parameter is set to permit_mynetworks,reject and reloads Postfix, which will stop unrestricted relaying. It achieves the remediation, though using postconf -e would be a cleaner, idempotent approach.

title: The Chronyd service is enabled
triage_reason: Enabling the chronyd service is a standard, low‑impact remediation that does not affect authentication, console access, or critical boot components. The provided script simply unmasks, starts, and enables the service, which is safe and reversible.
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q chrony; systemctl is-system-running; /usr/bin/systemctl unmask chronyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl enable chronyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl start chronyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl status chronyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl restart chronyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl status chronyd.service; sed -i '/^logfile /d' /etc/chrony.conf; /usr/bin/systemctl restart chronyd.service
review_feedback: The plan will enable and start chronyd by ensuring a minimal configuration exists, which satisfies the requirement that the service be enabled and runnable. However, overwriting /etc/chrony.conf without backup and without verifying existing custom settings is risky, and the plan does not enforce proper file permissions or ownership. Adding these safeguards would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: Blindly overwriting /etc/chrony.conf may discard existing custom NTP settings.; The plan does not back up the original configuration before modification.; File permissions and ownership for /etc/chrony.conf and /etc/chrony.keys are not set, which could expose sensitive data.; The static NTP pool may be inappropriate for some environments.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only creates a minimal chrony configuration and starts the chronyd service. This will not interrupt critical services like sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking, and will satisfy the requirement that chronyd be enabled and runnable. The main risk is overwriting any existing custom chrony settings without a backup and not setting file permissions, which could lead to loss of intended NTP configuration, but it does not cause immediate service outage or lockout.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of existing custom chrony configuration; Outbound NTP traffic to the public pool may be blocked by firewalls; If /etc/chrony.keys does not exist, chronyd may log warnings; Default logfile location may need appropriate permissions

title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured

review_feedback: The plan will add or uncomment a server/pool line and restart chronyd, which satisfies the requirement of having a remote time source.

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/chrony configuration files to add or modify the maxpoll parameter. This is a straightforward text substitution that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and the change can be validated quickly. The risk of locking out the system is negligible; the worst case is a temporary loss of time synchronization, which can be restored by restarting the service.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential NTP/chrony service interruption if configuration syntax is incorrect; Possible loss of accurate time until service is restarted
attempt_1_commands: ls -1 /etc/chrony.d; rpm -q chrony; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E '/^\s*(server|pool|peer)\b/ { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; ls -1 /etc/chrony.d; test -d /etc/chrony.d && echo exists || echo absent; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds a maxpoll value to server/pool/peer lines in /etc/chrony.conf and restarts chronyd, which will address the vulnerability for the primary configuration file.
review_concerns: Only /etc/chrony.conf is modified; additional files in /etc/chrony.d/ may contain server entries that would remain unpatched.; The maxpoll value is hard‑coded to 10 without confirming it matches organizational policy.
qa_verdict_reason: The sed command only appends a "maxpoll 10" to existing server/pool/peer directives that lack a maxpoll setting, leaving commented lines untouched. Adding maxpoll is a supported chrony option and using a value of 10 is within the allowed range. Restarting chronyd is the normal way to apply configuration changes and will cause only a brief interruption of time synchronization, not a service outage or SSH lockout.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of time synchronization while chronyd restarts; Potentially longer poll interval (maxpoll 10) may delay time adjustments

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the mode of the audit log file to a more restrictive setting (0600). This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect system boot, networking, or authentication mechanisms, and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; audit logs readable by unauthorized users; non‑compliance with STIG requirement
attempt_1_commands: awk -F'=' '/^[[:space:]]*log_file/ {gsub(/[[:space:]]/,"",$2); print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf; test -f /var/log/audit/audit.log && echo exists || echo missing; chmod 0600 /var/log/audit/audit.log
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the audit log file and applies a restrictive 0600 mode, which satisfies the requirement of 0640 or less permissive. It will resolve the current vulnerability.
review_concerns: The plan only changes permissions on the existing log file; newly created rotated logs may inherit insecure defaults.; No SELinux context handling; chmod may not be sufficient on SELinux-enabled systems.; The script does not verify that the file exists before attempting chmod, which could produce errors.; No logging or audit of the remediation action itself.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the audit log file’s mode to 0600 matches the security requirement and does not interfere with auditd’s ability to write the log (auditd runs as root). The operation does not affect SSH, networking, or other critical services, and no configuration files are modified. The only minor risk is that future rotated logs may be created with looser permissions, which does not cause immediate service disruption.
qa_side_effects: chmod on a non‑existent file may produce an error but does not affect system operation; subsequent log rotation may create new files with default, less‑restrictive permissions
